verses.text: The second lot came forth to Simeon, even for the tribe of the children of Simeon according to their families: their inheritance was within the inheritance of the children of Judah. They had in their inheritance Beer-sheba, Sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual, Balah, Azem, Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah, Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susah, Beth-lebaoth, Sharuhen — thirteen cities with their villages. Ain, Remmon, Ether, Ashan — four cities with their villages: and all the villages that were round about these cities to Baalath-beer, Ramath of the south. This was the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Simeon according to their families. Out of the portion of the children of Judah was the inheritance of the children of Simeon: for the part of the children of Judah was too much for them: therefore the children of Simeon had their inheritance within the inheritance of them. Guidance: Simeon gets no separate region — only cities embedded inside Judah's vast portion. Jacob's old curse on Simeon (Gen 49:7, 'I will divide them') is fulfilled gently: scattered, but among brothers. Judah's surplus becomes Simeon's home. Brotherhood reshapes prophecy from punishment into provision. The verse's quiet logic: when one tribe has more than it needs, the answer is not to expand into Canaanite territory but to share with a brother.

The third lot came up for the children of Zebulun according to their families: the border of their inheritance was unto Sarid. The border went up toward the sea, and Maralah, and reached to Dabbasheth, reached to the river that is before Jokneam; turned from Sarid eastward toward the sunrising unto the border of Chisloth-tabor, went out to Daberath, went up to Japhia. From thence passeth on along on the east to Gittah-hepher, to Ittah-kazin, went out to Remmon-methoar to Neah; the border compassed it on the north side to Hannathon: the outgoings thereof are in the valley of Jiphthah-el. Kattath, Nahallal, Shimron, Idalah, and Beth-lehem — twelve cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the children of Zebulun according to their families, these cities with their villages. Guidance: Zebulun draws the third lot. Jacob promised Zebulun would 'dwell at the haven of the sea' (Gen 49:13) — and so it does, on the western slope reaching toward the Mediterranean. The list includes Beth-lehem (not the famous Bethlehem in Judah; another one). And Gittah-hepher — better known later as Gath-hepher — will be the hometown of the prophet Jonah (2 Kings 14:25). Even a coastal mariner-tribe will produce a man who flees by boat. The geography seeds the story.

The fourth lot came out to Issachar, for the children of Issachar according to their families. Their border was toward Jezreel, Chesulloth, Shunem, Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath, Rabbith, Kishion, Abez, Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, Beth-pazzez: the coast reacheth to Tabor, Shahazimah, Beth-shemesh; the outgoings of their border were at Jordan: sixteen cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Issachar according to their families, the cities and their villages. Guidance: Issachar gets the great Jezreel Valley — Israel's breadbasket, also Israel's most fought-over plain. Mount Tabor (where Deborah and Barak will rally), Shunem (where Elisha will raise the widow's son), Jezreel itself (where Naboth's vineyard will be stolen by Jezebel) — all sit on Issachar's books. The tribe that Jacob said would be 'a strong ass bowing between two burdens' (Gen 49:14) gets the most productive — and most contested — ground in the land. Fruitfulness and conflict often share a property line.

The fifth lot came out for the tribe of the children of Asher according to their families. Their border was Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph, Alammelech, Amad, Misheal; reacheth to Carmel westward, and to Shihor-libnath; turneth toward the sunrising to Beth-dagon, reacheth to Zebulun, to the valley of Jiphthah-el toward the north side of Beth-emek, and Neiel, goeth out to Cabul on the left hand, and Hebron, and Rehob, and Hammon, and Kanah, even unto great Zidon; the coast turneth to Ramah, to the strong city Tyre; coast turneth to Hosah; outgoings at the sea from the coast to Achzib: Ummah, Aphek, and Rehob: twenty and two cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Asher according to their families, these cities with their villages. Guidance: Asher inherits the northwest coast: olive country, Phoenician trade routes, sight of Tyre and Sidon. Jacob said 'Asher's bread shall be fat, he shall yield royal dainties' (Gen 49:20) — and the territory matches. But the tribe never fully evicted the great port cities. The richest parcels often come with the most cosmopolitan compromises. Asher's wealth would eventually pull it toward its Phoenician neighbors instead of toward the LORD.

The fifth lot came out for the tribe of the children of Asher according to their families. Their border was Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph, Alammelech, Amad, Misheal; reacheth to Carmel westward, and to Shihor-libnath; turneth toward the sunrising to Beth-dagon, reacheth to Zebulun, to the valley of Jiphthah-el toward the north side of Beth-emek, and Neiel, goeth out to Cabul on the left hand, and Hebron, and Rehob, and Hammon, and Kanah, even unto great Zidon; the coast turneth to Ramah, to the strong city Tyre; coast turneth to Hosah; outgoings at the sea from the coast to Achzib: Ummah, Aphek, and Rehob: twenty and two cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Asher according to their families, these cities with their villages. Guidance: Asher inherits the northwest coast: olive country, Phoenician trade routes, sight of Tyre and Sidon. Jacob said 'Asher's bread shall be fat, he shall yield royal dainties' (Gen 49:20) — and the territory matches. But the tribe never fully evicted the great port cities. The richest parcels often come with the most cosmopolitan compromises. Asher's wealth would eventually pull it toward its Phoenician neighbors instead of toward the LORD.

The sixth lot came out to the children of Naphtali, even for the children of Naphtali according to their families. Their coast was from Heleph, from Allon to Zaanannim, Adami, Nekeb, Jabneel, unto Lakum; the outgoings thereof were at Jordan: the coast turned westward to Aznoth-tabor, went out to Hukkok, reached to Zebulun on the south side, and reached to Asher on the west side, and to Judah upon Jordan toward the sunrising. The fenced cities are Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Chinnereth, Adamah, Ramah, Hazor, Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor, Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, Beth-shemesh — nineteen cities with their villages. This is the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Naphtali according to their families, the cities and their villages. Guidance: Naphtali wraps around the Sea of Galilee (Chinnereth). Hazor is on the list — though already burned in chapter 11, the city site is here re-assigned. Kedesh of Naphtali will be Barak's home (Judges 4) and one of the cities of refuge. Centuries later Isaiah 9 will name 'the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali' as the place where 'the people that walked in darkness have seen a great light' — Matthew 4 will identify that light as Jesus, ministering on this very ground. The lot drawn for Naphtali today will hold the footsteps of Christ tomorrow.

The seventh lot came out for the tribe of the children of Dan according to their families. The coast of their inheritance was Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh, Shaalabbin, Ajalon, Jethlah, Elon, Thimnathah, Ekron, Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath, Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon, Me-jarkon, Rakkon, with the border before Japho. The coast of the children of Dan went out too little for them: therefore the children of Dan went up to fight against Leshem, took it, smote it with the edge of the sword, possessed it, dwelt therein, called Leshem Dan, after the name of Dan their father. Guidance: Dan gets the narrow band west of Benjamin — including Zorah and Eshtaol where Samson will be born. But the territory turns out too tight, and Dan migrates north to a town called Leshem (also Laish, Judges 18), rebranding it Dan. The relocation has a darker side; the Judges narrative will show Dan stealing a Levite's idols on the way. Still, the lot worked: Dan ends up giving its name to the northernmost city of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'). Sometimes a too-small inheritance becomes a too-far migration; geography always matters less than what one carries on the journey.

The seventh lot came out for the tribe of the children of Dan according to their families. The coast of their inheritance was Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh, Shaalabbin, Ajalon, Jethlah, Elon, Thimnathah, Ekron, Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath, Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon, Me-jarkon, Rakkon, with the border before Japho. The coast of the children of Dan went out too little for them: therefore the children of Dan went up to fight against Leshem, took it, smote it with the edge of the sword, possessed it, dwelt therein, called Leshem Dan, after the name of Dan their father. Guidance: Dan gets the narrow band west of Benjamin — including Zorah and Eshtaol where Samson will be born. But the territory turns out too tight, and Dan migrates north to a town called Leshem (also Laish, Judges 18), rebranding it Dan. The relocation has a darker side; the Judges narrative will show Dan stealing a Levite's idols on the way. Still, the lot worked: Dan ends up giving its name to the northernmost city of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'). Sometimes a too-small inheritance becomes a too-far migration; geography always matters less than what one carries on the journey.

When they had made an end of dividing the land for inheritance by their coasts, the children of Israel gave an inheritance to Joshua son of Nun among them: according to the word of the LORD they gave him the city which he asked, Timnath-serah in mount Ephraim: he built the city, and dwelt therein. These are the inheritances, which Eleazar the priest, and Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the fathers of the tribes of the children of Israel, divided for an inheritance by lot in Shiloh before the LORD, at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation. So they made an end of dividing the country. Guidance: Joshua is the last to take a portion, after every tribe has been settled. Only after his people are housed does he ask for his own home — Timnath-serah, a town in his own tribe Ephraim. A leader who served last gets served last, and is content with it. He has to build the city; the inheritance is an unfinished site, not a furnished palace. And the work of dividing is signed off again — Eleazar the priest, Joshua the general, the tribal heads, all at Shiloh, all before the LORD. The covenant transaction has the same three signatures throughout the book.
